text: Pick up a paperclip. Hold it in your palm. Feel how light it is.
visual: Close-up of a hand, palm up. A single small steel paperclip rests in the center, glinting softly. Label fades in: '1 gram.'

text: One gram of metal. Nothing. You could lose it in a couch cushion.
visual: The hand tilts. The paperclip slides off and falls into the cushions of a sofa, disappearing between them.

text: Now here's a sentence that sounds insane.
visual: Black frame. A small text marker fades in: 'a sentence that sounds insane.'

text: That paperclip — if you could open it up the right way — has the same energy inside it as the bomb that destroyed Hiroshima.
visual: Split frame. Left: the small paperclip resting on a fingertip. Right: an aerial silhouette of a city skyline with a mushroom cloud rising over it. A faint equals sign hovers between them.

text: Not a piece of one. The whole bomb. From one gram of bent wire.
visual: The paperclip on the left brightens. The mushroom cloud on the right brightens. The equals sign between them glows.

text: How is that possible? Where is all that energy hiding?
visual: The frame fades down. The paperclip alone remains, surrounded by darkness. A question mark hovers above it.

text: To answer that, forget physics for a minute. Look at a glass of water.
visual: The paperclip dissolves. A simple glass of water appears on a table, viewed from the side.

text: You can freeze it. You get ice. You can melt the ice. You get water back.
visual: The glass shifts state in three frames: liquid water → solid ice cube → liquid water again. A small temperature gauge moves with each change.

text: Same stuff. Different state. Hard, then soft. Locked, then free.
visual: Two labels appear side by side beneath the glass: 'ice' and 'water,' connected by a double-headed arrow.

text: Now imagine this — but bigger. Much bigger.
visual: The glass shrinks. A larger circular badge appears in its place, faintly glowing.

text: Matter is frozen energy.
visual: Inside the badge, two labels appear linked by a double-headed arrow: 'matter' and 'energy.' Below them in smaller text: 'same stuff, different state.'

text: Everything solid — the paperclip, your phone, your hand — is energy that got compressed into a shape that holds still.
visual: Quick visual triplet: a paperclip, a phone, an open hand. Each one briefly dissolves into shimmering particles of light, then re-forms into its solid shape.

text: And the energy didn't go anywhere when it froze. It's still in there. Just locked.
visual: Return to the paperclip alone. A faint inner glow pulses through it, suggesting hidden contents.

text: So now the real question. If matter is frozen energy — how much energy is frozen in a gram?
visual: The paperclip sits in the center. A small label fades in beside it: '1 gram = ? energy.'

text: Einstein answered that in one line.
visual: The label fades. The equation appears in elegant typography in the center of the frame: E = mc²

text: Energy equals mass — times something called c squared.
visual: The 'E,' the 'm,' and the 'c²' each light up briefly as they're named.

text: Most equations have a polite little number for the conversion. This one doesn't.
visual: The 'm' dims. The 'c²' remains glowing in the center.

text: c is the speed of light. Three hundred million meters per second. The fastest thing in the universe.
visual: Above the equation, an animation: a small dot traces from one side of the frame to the other almost instantly. A label fades in: 'c = 300,000,000 m/s.'

text: And we don't multiply mass by c. We multiply it by c times c.
visual: The 'c²' separates into 'c × c' floating beside the equation.

text: Three hundred million times three hundred million. Roughly a hundred thousand trillion.
visual: Two large numbers appear: 300,000,000 × 300,000,000. They collapse into a single result: '≈ 90,000,000,000,000,000.'

text: That's the exchange rate. One gram of mass, fully cashed in, gives you a hundred thousand trillion joules of energy.
visual: Split frame returns. Left: a 1-gram paperclip on a small scale. Right: a tall bar labeled '≈ 9 × 10¹⁶ joules' rising off the top of the frame.

text: That's why a paperclip is a bomb. Not because the paperclip is special. Because c² is enormous.
visual: The 'c²' from the equation grows large in the center, dwarfing the paperclip beside it.

text: Anything with mass is carrying this. Your phone. Your coffee. You.
visual: Quick cuts: a phone, a coffee cup, a person — each briefly haloed with a faint inner glow.

text: You are walking around with the energy of thousands of bombs inside you. Frozen. Locked. Quiet.
visual: A silhouette of a person stands calmly in the center of the frame. A soft inner glow pulses through the silhouette.

text: We don't normally have keys to that lock. Burning wood doesn't open it. Lighting gasoline doesn't open it. Even a regular chemical bomb barely cracks it.
visual: Three small icons appear and dissolve: a campfire, a gasoline flame, a small explosion. Each one barely scratches a large padlock icon in the corner.

text: A nuclear reaction is the first thing humans built that opens the lock — and even then, only a little. Hiroshima released the energy of less than one gram of matter.
visual: The padlock cracks slightly. A small portion of light spills out — and that small portion becomes the mushroom cloud from earlier.

text: Less than one gram. Out of an entire bomb. The rest of it just sat there as matter, frozen, untouched.
visual: A bomb shape appears with most of it shaded in solid color, and a tiny sliver highlighted in glowing light. Label on the sliver: '< 1 gram.' Label on the rest: 'still frozen.'

text: So pick up the paperclip again.
visual: Callback. Return to the opening shot. The hand, palm up, with the paperclip resting in the center.

text: It's not a piece of metal. It's a sealed container. Holding the energy of a city.
visual: The paperclip in the palm starts to pulse with a soft inner glow. The glow holds, quietly, while the hand stays still.

text: We just don't know how to open it. Yet.
visual: Hold on the glowing paperclip in the palm. The glow softens. The last word lingers as the frame slowly fades.
